SUMMARY:
Extreme hydro-meteorological events drive hazardous hydrologic and geomorphic responses, such as floods, landslides, and debris flows,

*red apples*  
returns results with all terms like:

*"red apples"*  
returns results matching exactly like:  
*Anthocyanin biosynthesis in **red apples***

Exclude a term with -: term1 -term2  
*apples -red*  
returns results containing ***apples*** but not ***red***:  
*Malic acid in green **apples***

--- SOURCE 2: Climate change impacts on hydrometeorological and river ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094

SUMMARY:
A few studies have investigated climate change impacts on the hydrological system in Quito and Ecuador (Mora et al., 2014, Muñoz et al., 2014, SEI, 2020). So far, the analysis of climate change impacts on the river flow extremes using the new scenarios and models from the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project phase 6 (CMIP6) has not been conducted yet for the city of Quito or even the broader region of Ecuador. In this context, this research aims to analyse the impacts of climate change in both the near and far future on hydrological and meteorological extremes in Quito using state-of-the-art global climate models (GCM) and four future scenarios based on the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs).

FULL CONTENT:
Climate change impacts on hydrometeorological and river hydrological extremes in Quito, Ecuador - ScienceDirect

Climate change impacts on hydrometeorological and river hydrological extremes in Quito, Ecuador
===============================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Santiago X.Núñez Mejía a b, Santiago Mendoza Paz c, Hossein Tabari d e, Patrick Willems c

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejrh.2023.101522](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejrh.2023.101522 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2214581823002094&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Climate change impacts on hydrometeorological extremes are evaluated in Quito. 
*   •Spatial and temporal downscaling is applied to an ensemble of GCMs. 
*   •Short-duration extreme precipitation events are expected to be more intense. 
*   •Low flows are projected to decrease in the San Pedro River. 
*   •Climate models are the dominant source of uncertainty. 

Abstract
--------

### Study region

Quito, Ecuador

### Study focus

The study region faces two water-related challenges, which, to date, have only been studied to a minimal extent: extreme precipitation events and water shortage in the dry season. This study investigates the current conditions and future changes in short-duration events, low river discharges and associated uncertainties. Daily precipitation and temperature projections from 19 state-of-the-art global climate models (CMIP6) and four future scenarios (SSP1–2.6, SSP2–4.5, SSP3–7.0 and SSP5–8.5) are downscaled with delta change and more advanced [quantile](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/quantile "Learn more about quantile from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") perturbation methodologies. Temporal disaggregation is applied to obtain sub-daily precipitation with five extreme value distributions (EVDs). Three conceptual hydrological models are implemented to quantify the impacts on river flows. Variance decomposition is applied to estimate the uncertainty share of climate models, hydrological models, and EVDs in the results.

### New hydrological insights

The results indicate an intensification of extreme precipitation events, with short-duration events expected to be more intense by up to 30% in the near future (2021–2050) and up to 60% in the far future (2070–2099). The river [peak discharges](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/peak-discharge "Learn more about peak discharges from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are projected to increase by 5–20% and 10–50% in the near and far future, respectively. On the contrary, the low river flows are projected to decrease by 0–13% and 0–30% for the respective time horizons. Overall, climate models are the dominant source of uncertainty.

Keywords
--------

Climate change

Statistical downscaling

Temporal downscaling

IDF curves

Sub-daily precipitation extremes

Conceptual hydrological models

1. Introduction
---------------

Climate change represents a major threat to the world, with higher risks for vulnerable regions like South America. Socioeconomic factors such as poverty, inequity, and limited access to essential services, combined with the uneven distribution of water resources increase the region's susceptibility to climate impacts ([CAF, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib3)). According to the sixth assessment report of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), the emerging risks associated with climate change are larger in urban areas and cities ([Dodman et al., in press](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib11)) due to the high concentration of population and assets as well as the extensive interdependencies of infrastructure systems ([Penny et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib36)).

Quito, Ecuador's capital and most populated city, has been experiencing climate change due to [global warming](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/global-warming "Learn more about global warming from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Over the period 1960–2010, the annual mean temperature in the city increased by 1.1°C, while annual precipitation increased by 13% ([Secretaría de Ambiente del DMQ and C40, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib41)). The Action Plan for Quito on Climate Change ([Secretaría de Ambiente del DMQ and C40, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib41)) identified two main challenges related to water resources in the city: prolonged absence of precipitation inducing water stress, and intensification of short-duration rain episodes that cause landslides and urban pluvial [flooding](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/flood "Learn more about flooding from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

The risk of water stress in Quito is explained by increasing water demand mainly due to population growth, and decreasing surface [water supply](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/water-supply "Learn more about water supply from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from rivers due to climate change induced alterations in precipitation patterns. In contrast, pluvial flooding in the urban area is increasing, threatening human settlements. For instance, between February and March 2021, 136 emergencies were reported due to extreme rain events in Pichincha ([SNGR, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib43)) and 220 flooding events were observed in the 2018 winter in Quito ([Quito Informa, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib40)). This raises the need to analyse water availability in the supplying catchments of Quito City and assess the occurrence of extreme precipitation events in the urban district.

A few studies have investigated [climate change impacts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/climate-change-impact "Learn more about climate change impacts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on the hydrological system in Quito and Ecuador ([Mora et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib32), [Muñoz et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib33), [SEI, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib42)). However, some limitations of these previous studies could be identified: (1) the limited focus on the [Paramo](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/paramo "Learn more about Paramo from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") region without considering the direct impacts on the urban area, (2) the use of few climate models from previous generations (CMIP3 and CMIP5), (3) the inclusion of only one or two climate change scenarios (RCP4.5 and/or only RCP8.5), (4) the low performance of the calibrated hydrological models due to data scarcity and (5) missing or incomplete uncertainty estimates on the hydrological impacts. The uncertainty analysis of future projections in the tropical regions is essential as the projection uncertainty in these regions is large, to the extent that the regions were identified as uncertainty hotspots worldwide ([Tabari et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib48)). So far, the analysis of climate change impacts on the river flow extremes using the new scenarios and models from the [Coupled Model Intercomparison Project phase 6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/coupled-model-intercomparison-project-phase-6 "Learn more about Coupled Model Intercomparison Project phase 6 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CMIP6) has not been conducted yet for the city of Quito or even the broader region of Ecuador.

In this context, this research aims to analyse the impacts of climate change in both the near and far future on hydrological and meteorological extremes in Quito using state-of-the-art global climate models (GCM) and four future scenarios based on the [Shared Socioeconomic Pathways](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/shared-socioeconomic-pathways "Learn more about Shared Socioeconomic Pathways from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (SSPs). The research methodology involves two key steps. First, a two-step spatial-temporal statistical downscaling method is applied based on the [Quantile](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/quantile "Learn more about Quantile from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") Perturbation approach (QP) and the distribution-based temporal disaggregation to a set of 19 CMIP6 GCMs of daily precipitation. This leads to projected changes in the intensity-duration-frequency (IDF) curves fitted with five theoretical distributions. Second, the changes in runoff and discharge are assessed for one of the supplying catchments, the San Pedro River catchment, which is modelled with three lumped-conceptual hydrological models (NAM, GR4J, and VHM). The impacts on low and high flow extremes (directly related toflood and water availability) are quantified. Throughout the research, the involved uncertainties are considered and quantified with the variance decomposition method by including a range of possible futures according to different climate models, future scenarios, extreme value distributions and hydrological models.

2. Materials and methods
------------------------

### 2.1. Study area

The research is carried out in two distinct areas: the urban district of the city of Quito and the San Pedro catchment ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#fig0005)). In the former, the focus is on investigating the impacts on the IDF curves and sub-daily precipitation events. In the latter, the interest is the low, medium, and peak daily river discharges.

![Image 12: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2214581823002094-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (362KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2214581823002094-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (362KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2214581823002094-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Stations with meteorological observations and the RAIN4PE/MAATE grid over the study area. The stations displayed in red are the ones with sub-daily maximum precipitation observations and monthly variables for reference evapotranspiration calculation.

Quito is geographically located on a plateau between the eastern and western [Andes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/andes "Learn more about Andes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") Mountain range, at a medium elevation of 2850 meters above sea level (m.a.s.l). These topographical characteristics generate different climate zones within the city, ranging from a [temperate climate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/temperate-climate "Learn more about temperate climate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the valleys to a humid climate in the eastern and western foothills, and a highland climate in the areas above 3000 m.a.s.l. The local [orographic effects](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/orographic-effect "Learn more about orographic effects from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") significantly influence precipitation, leading to the distinction of three zones, the dry inter-Andean region (554 mm/year), the inter-Andean zone (960 mm/year), and the rainy zone (1400 mm/year) ([Secretaría de Ambiente del DMQ and C40, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib41)).

The urban centre, surrounded by mountains and brooks, lies close to ravines, making it a flood-prone area that can experience landslides and flash floods during extreme precipitation events. For its [drinking water supply](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/drinking-water-supply "Learn more about drinking water supply from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), Quito's urban area relies on some tributaries of the Guayllabamba River and other important tributaries in the eastern region (Napo province), which are not considered in this research. In particular, the San Pedro River is part of the southern conveyance system, and supplies around 1.02 m 3/s of [drinking water](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/potable-water "Learn more about drinking water from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to the city ([De Bievre, Coello, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib9)), mainly for the Amaguaña and Machachi towns.

The San Pedro catchment is located in the south of the urban zone and belongs to the conservation area of the FONAG-EPMAPS, a public-private fund dedicated to preserving water-supplying catchments. The catchment covers a total area of 752 km 2, with approximately 45% covered by natural vegetation, including [paramo](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/paramo "Learn more about paramo from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), natural forest, and bushes ([FONAG, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib16)). The remaining area is occupied by various land use classes such as cropland, urban settlements, and water bodies. The average annual rainfall in the catchment is 1200 mm ([Fernandes-Palomino et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib14)), with a dry season from June to September and the rainiest months being March and April. Moreover, the mean temperature in the catchment varies from 12 °C in the highest area to 15 °C in the lowest points.

### 2.2. Observation datasets

Daily observations of precipitation, mean, minimum, and maximum temperatures from 1981 to 2010 were obtained from the National Meteorology and Hydrology Institute of Ecuador (INAMHI). Among the meteorological stations, three (La Tola, Izobamba, and Iñaquito) provided records for all four variables spanning over 30 years, while 11 stations only had precipitation data available ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#tbl0005)). The selection of a period of at least 30 years aligns with the guidelines of the [World Meteorological Organization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/world-meteorological-organization "Learn more about World Meteorological Organization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") as it is considered to be larger than a natural [climate cycle](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/climate-cycle "Learn more about climate cycle from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Willems et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib57), [WMO, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib58)). The geographical distribution of these stations is presented in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#fig0005).

Table 1. Stations with daily meteorological variables for the 1981–2010 period.

| No. | Station | Name | Variables (Daily obs.) | Period | Missing Data (%) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | M002 | La Tola | Tmed, Tmin, Tmax, Prec | 1981–2010 | 1.7/1.5/1.5/0.6 |
| 2 | M003 | Izobamba | Tmed, Tmin, Tmax, Prec | 1981–2010 | 1.3/0.7/0.8/0.01 |
| 3 | M024 | Inamhi-Iñaquito | Tmed, Tmin, Tmax, Prec | 1981–2010 | 5.7/4.6/4.8/3.99 |
| 4 | M113 | Uyumbicho | Prec | 1981–2010 | 10.33 |
| 5 | M335 | La Chorrera | Prec | 1981–2010 | 8.49 |
| 6 | M343 | El Quinche | Prec | 1981–2010 | 1.42 |
| 7 | M345 | Calderon | Prec | 1981–2010 | 3.21 |
| 8 | M346 | Yaruqui | Prec | 1981–2010 | 1.22 |
| 9 | M353 | Rumipamba | Prec | 1981–2010 | 6.58 |
| 10 | M354 | San Juan | Prec | 1981–2010 | 4.02 |
| 11 | M357 | Canal 10 Tv | Prec | 1981–2010 | 2.28 |
| 12 | M358 | Calacali | Prec | 1981–2010 | 1.15 |
| 13 | M361 | Nono | Prec | 1981–2010 | 2.06 |
| 14 | M364 | Loreto Pedregal | Prec | 1981–2010 | 2.21 |

To obtain the historical IDF curves, sub-daily records of the maximum intensities in 5-minute intervals (annual maxima) for the period 1981–2010 were also provided by INAMHI for the three of the afore mentioned stations (La Tola, Izobamba, and Iñaquito). These stations are located within Quito's urban area as depicted in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#fig0005). Additionally, monthly observations for the period 1990–2010 were collected for sunshine hours, relative humidity, mean, minimum and maximum temperature, precipitation, and pan evaporation for the same three stations in the study area. These observations are considered for calculating daily reference [evapotranspiration](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/evapotranspiration "Learn more about evapotranspiration from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (ETo).

As there is no [rain gauge](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/rain-gage "Learn more about rain gauge from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with at least 30 years of observations present inside the San Pedro catchment, a novelty of this study is the application of the RAIN4PE gridded precipitation dataset with a 0.1° resolution ([Fernandes-Palomino et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib14)). The RAIN4PE dataset is a recent product obtained by merging ground-based observations with [ERA5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/fifth-generation-of-ecmwf-atmospheric-reanalysis-of-the-global-climate "Learn more about ERA5 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") reanalysis ([Hersbach et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib22)) and satellite-based precipitation from the Climate Hazards Group InfraRed Precipitation, CHIRPS ([Funk et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib17)). The authors used the terrain elevation and the random forest regression method to interpolate the values in the grid. This product has the advantage that it was hydrologically corrected in some areas using [streamflow](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/streamflow "Learn more about streamflow from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") data. This correction involved the application of reverse hydrology with a calibrated SWAT model ([Fernandes-Palomino et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib14)).

In the same way, temperature observations in the catchment were obtained from a gridded dataset with a 0.1° resolution, encompassing maximum, minimum, and mean temperature values ([UNDP, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib52)). It was created by merging ground-based observations with the Climate Hazards Group InfraRed Temperature, CHIRTS ([Funk et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib18)). To obtain a single value for the entire catchment, a weighted average based on the area of the catchment covered by each grid cell was applied.

Regarding the river flow observations, data from one hydrological station (H0159) was provided by UNDP (as processed by [Bastidas et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib2)). Daily mean flow observations for the period 1985–2010 at the San Pedro catchment were used to calibrate and validate the hydrological models. The time series of discharge observations were homogenised and filled with values obtained with the GR4J conceptual hydrological model ([Bastidas et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#bib2)). The [catchment area](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/catchment-area "Learn more about catchment area from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") upstream of the H0159 station is 363 km 2, representing 48% of the San Pedro catchment area.

### 2.3. Climate change projections

This study makes use of the latest generation of global climate models (GCMs), from the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project – Phase 6 (CMIP6). The historical period in this research is defined as 1981–2010. The climate change signals are evaluated for the near- (2021–2050) and far- (2070–2099) future under the four most plausible scenarios in Tier 1: SSP1–2.6, SSP2–4.5, SSP3–7.0, and SSP5–8.5. This research utilises all the available GCMs with daily temperature and precipitation projections ([Table 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214581823002094#tbl0010)). Since GCM evapotranspiration projections were available at a monthly scale, with only one model containing daily projections, the ETo projections had to be derived based on temperature projections and the Hargreaves method.

Table 2. CMIP6 GCMs and number of runs per model used for future daily precipitation and ETo (estimated from the daily mean, minimum, and maximum temperature) analysis (95 runs).

| No. | GCM Model | Spatial resolution (Lon x Lat) | Historical | SSP 1–2.6 | SSP 2–4.5 | SSP 3–7.0 | SSP 5–8.5 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | ACCESS-CM2 | 1.875° x 1.25° | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 2 | ACCESS-ESM1–5 | 1.875° x 1.25° | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 3 | CMCC-ESM2 | 1.25° x 0.95° | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 4 | CanESM5 | 2.8° x 2.8° | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 5 | Ec-Earth3 | 0.7° x 0.7° | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 6 | EC-Earth3-Veg | 0.7° x 0.7° | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 7 | EC-Earth3-Veg-LR | 1.125° x 1.125° | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 8 | FGOALS-g3 | 2° x 2° | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 9 | INM-CM4–8 | 2° x 1.5° | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 10 | INM-CM5–0 | 2° x 1.5° | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 11 | IPSL-CM6A-LR | 2.5°

enriched_points.expanded_content: Climate change is causing hydro-meteorological events like floods and droughts to occur more frequently and with greater intensity, as evidenced by rising global temperatures and altered weather patterns.
Recent events, such as the devastating floods in Quito (2021) and increased hurricane activity, highlight the escalating impact of climate change on communities globally.

Communities, particularly in developing regions, are increasingly vulnerable due to inadequate infrastructure, poverty, and limited access to resources, exacerbating the effects of hydro-meteorological hazards.

The rise in hydro-meteorological hazards has significant global implications, including economic losses, displacement of populations, and increased humanitarian crises, underscoring the need for international cooperation.
There is an urgent need for communities and governments to enhance preparedness and resilience through better planning, early warning systems, and sustainable development practices to mitigate risks.
